commentary:
## Introduction: The Significance of Surah Al-Asr

The companions of the Prophet ﷺ held this surah in such high regard that they would recite it to each other whenever they met, before parting ways. As narrated in Tabarani, no two companions would meet without reciting Surah Al-Asr to one another before saying their farewells. This practice demonstrates the centrality of this surah in the lives of the first generation of Muslims.

Imam Shafi'i (RA) made two remarkable statements about this surah. He said: "If people just contemplated this surah, it would encompass everything for them." On another occasion, he stated: "If Allah ﷻ had sent nothing down except this surah, it would have been enough for humanity."

This surah fundamentally changed my own relationship with the Quran. Everything I have done in my study of the Quran over the past 17-18 years goes back to the transformative experience of understanding Al-Asr. It redefined my view of myself, my life, reality itself, and the entire Quran. For anyone developing a lifelong relationship with the Quran, there is no better starting point than this surah.

## The Divine Sequence: Context Within the Quran

Surah Al-Asr is the fifth in a group of six surahs, from Al-Zalzalah (99) onward. Understanding its placement illuminates its message. The progression is deliberate:

**Surah Al-Zalzalah** establishes that whoever does even an atom's weight of good or evil will see it. The emphasis is on *seeing* - the outward manifestation of deeds.

**Surah Al-'Adiyat** goes deeper: "What is in the hearts will be retrieved" (حُصِّلَ مَا فِي الصُّدُورِ). Now both the outward and inward dimensions of deeds are revealed - what we did and what was in our hearts when we did it.

**Surah Al-Qari'ah** follows logically: after the outward and inward have been evaluated, these deeds are ready to be weighed: "Whoever's scales are heavy... whoever's scales are light..."

**Surah At-Takathur** addresses the root problem: "Competition in worldly gain has distracted you." We're obsessed with getting more and better, blind to what truly has weight.

This brings us to **Surah Al-Asr**. After discussing greed, false standards, and the weighing of deeds, Allah ﷻ makes His declaration about human loss and the path to avoid it.

## The Ayah of the Oath

### وَالْعَصْرِ

**"By time as it passes away..."**

### The Significance of Taking an Oath

When Allah ﷻ takes an oath, multiple dimensions of meaning emerge:

**First**, oaths function as testimony - a solemn validation that what follows is absolutely true.

**Second**, oaths address skepticism. When someone feels the need to say "I swear," they're acknowledging their audience may not readily believe them. Allah ﷻ is removing doubt before the statement is even made.

**Third**, oaths can express anger. "I swear to God, you better stop" - the oath itself manifests displeasure. This surah opens with divine anger already expressed.

**Fourth**, in Arabic specifically, oaths capture attention - like a news alert demanding: "Listen up! This is urgent, and you don't have much time."

**Fifth**, unique to Quranic usage, whatever Allah ﷻ swears by becomes evidence for what He's about to say. Time itself will substantiate the coming statement.

### The Meaning of Al-'Asr

The word "al-'asr" (العصر) is not the typical Arabic word for time. The standard words are "zaman" (زمان), "waqt" (وقت), or "hin" (حين). Allah ﷻ chose a specific, profound word.

**Linguistically**, 'asr comes from 'asara (عصر), meaning to squeeze or extract - like juicing fruit, squeezing grapes, or pressing olives. The word describes something dripping out slowly, drop by drop. Applied to time, it evokes life being squeezed out of us, moment by moment, drop by drop.

**Secondly**, 'asr describes powerful tornadoes - unstoppable forces that destroy everything in their path. Time is like this: immensely powerful, impossible to halt, destroying youth, health, possessions - everything eventually falls before it.

**Thirdly**, 'asr refers to the late afternoon prayer time - when the sun begins its descent, its light weakening, being "squeezed down" into the horizon. That brief window between afternoon and sunset passes almost imperceptibly; before you realize it, Maghrib has arrived. This captures time's deceptive speed.

**Fourthly**, 'asr means to restrain or imprison - to hold something and not let it go. We are surrounded by time, held captive by it, unable to escape its grip as it squeezes the life from us.

**Finally**, Hameeduddin Farahi noted that 'asr specifically refers to time that has *already been lost* - "the good old days," time you can't recover. This creates dual urgency: regret for time already wasted, and desperate awareness of the little time remaining.

By swearing by al-'asr, Allah ﷻ draws attention to time that:
- Is dripping away, drop by drop
- Cannot be stopped, like a tornado
- Will destroy everything in its path
- Is passing faster than we realize
- Has largely already been lost
- Leaves us very little remaining

And yet, this same time that imprisons us also protects us - if used correctly. The greatest liability we have is time, but the greatest protection against loss is also the proper use of time.

### Time and Worship

Every primary act of worship returns to respect for precise times:

- Five daily prayers at specific times
- Ramadan in a specific month
- Hajj in specific days
- Fajr prayer: "The Quran recited at dawn is witnessed" (إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كَانَ مَشْهُودًا)
- Seeking forgiveness at dawn: "Those who seek forgiveness in the early hours" (وَالْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحَارِ)

Why seek forgiveness in the morning before you've even sinned? Because you acknowledge before Allah ﷻ: "No matter how hard I try, I will probably make mistakes. Before I even make them, I seek Your forgiveness." You don't wait until you've messed up - you maintain humility from the very start.

The same time that we're losing in life gives us opportunities to make gains. Taking advantage of specific times, particular seasons, blessed nights - this is how we convert loss into profit.

## The Declaration of Loss

### إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ

**"Truly, every single human being is drowning in loss."**

This is the thesis of the surah - and it's a dark thesis. Every single human being, without exception, is in a state of loss. This applies to all of us: Muslims, non-Muslims, those who came before, those who will come after. No one escapes this verdict.

### The Emphatic Structure

The statement employs multiple devices of emphasis:

**First**: The oath itself (وَالْعَصْرِ) already creates emphasis.

**Second**: The particle "inna" (إِنَّ) - "truly, no doubt, certainly" - removes all skepticism.

**Third**: The nominal sentence structure (الجملة الاسمية) in Arabic is the strongest way to state a fact.

**Fourth**: "Al-insan" (الْإِنسَانَ) - *the* human being, meaning each and every one, not just "people" in general. When you say "people are in trouble," everyone thinks you mean someone else. When you say "*the* human being" - every single individual - no one can deflect responsibility.

**Fifth**: The word "insan" itself contains criticism. Unlike "bashar" (بشر) or "naas" (ناس), "insan" emphasizes humanity's emotional, forgetful nature. We are creatures of sentiment who quickly forget uncomfortable truths.

**Sixth**: The letter "lam" (ل) in "lafi" (لَفِي) adds yet another layer of oath-like emphasis.

**Seventh**: "Fi khusr" (فِي خُسْرٍ) - *in* loss, meaning already drowning in it, not merely heading toward it.

**Eighth**: The indefinite noun "khusr" (خُسْرٍ) with tanwin suggests: all kinds of loss, any degree of loss, constant loss.

It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying: "I swear, I swear again - no doubt about it - every single person without exception is drowning in various forms of continuous loss."

### Understanding Human Beings in the Quran

To understand why this declaration is so severe, we must examine how Allah ﷻ describes human beings throughout the Quran. The word "insan" (إنسان) appears 56 times. After studying all these occurrences, I'm convinced that every instance further elaborates the reality declared in Surah Al-Asr: humans are in loss.

These can be grouped into categories:

#### Category 1: What Humans Were Created From

- "We created the human being from dried clay" (مِن صَلْصَالٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ) - Al-Hijr 15:26
- "From a drop of fluid, then he became argumentative" (مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ) - An-Nahl 16:4
- "Was there not a time when the human being was nothing worth mentioning?" (هَلْ أَتَىٰ عَلَى الْإِنسَانِ حِينٌ مِّنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُن شَيْئًا مَّذْكُورًا) - Al-Insan 76:1
- "Let the human being consider what he was created from - from a fluid that gushes forth" (فَلْيَنظُرِ الْإِنسَانُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ * خُلِقَ مِن مَّاءٍ دَافِقٍ) - At-Tariq 86:5-6

The pattern is clear: We were made from dirt and fluid - both humiliating origins. Dirt represents worthlessness, failure. A patch of dirt is barren until life grows from it. Fluid (the drop that becomes a human) is something embarrassing, hidden, shameful.

**Why does Allah ﷻ repeatedly remind us of these humble origins?**

Because we forget. We become arrogant, argumentative, ungrateful, entitled. We lose sight of where we came from. If you forget your past, you forget your responsibilities and ruin your future. Remembering our origins should keep us humble and grateful - the opposite of loss.

#### Category 2: How Humans Were Created (Our Inherent Weaknesses)

Allah ﷻ gave us certain limitations by design:

- **"The human being was created weak"** (خُلِقَ الْإِنسَانُ ضَعِيفًا) - An-Nisa 4:28. This follows Allah's statement: "Allah wants to lighten your burden." He gave us laws knowing we're weak, so we can handle them. But it also means we're too weak to see the bigger picture - we think His laws make life harder when they actually make it easier.

- **"The human being was created anxious, panicky, impatient"** (إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا) - Al-Ma'arij 70:19. We want everything immediately. When something good happens, we're overly excited. When something bad happens, we lose all hope. We're emotionally reactionary.

- **"The human being was created with a sense of urgency"** (خُلِقَ الْإِنسَانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ) - Al-Anbiya 21:37. We rush into things. We want results now. Everything must be quick, fast, immediate. Young people can't wait to get married. Parents can't wait for grandchildren. We're buying houses, getting careers - rush, rush, rush.

- **"We created the human being in toil"** (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي كَبَدٍ) - Al-Balad 90:4. Life comes with constant stress, labor, difficulty - physical or mental or emotional. The farmer breaks his back physically; the wealthy man can't sleep without pills. Everyone has their form of exhaustion. We were programmed this way. If you think "one day I'll have nothing to worry about," keep wishing - that's not how we were made.

But here's the mercy: **For every weakness Allah ﷻ mentions, He provides a remedy.**

- Weakness? Study and reflect on Allah's words, and you'll become stronger (يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ).
- Impatient and reactionary? Pray consistently. Prayer gives you control over time and temptations (إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ).
- Want everything too quickly? Accept that some things come in their time according to Allah's plan (سَأُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِي فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ).
- Exhausted from life's toil? Remember the Most Merciful created you. He knows every whisper of your soul, closer to you than your jugular vein (وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ).

Finally, despite all these weaknesses: **"We created the human being in the best possible form"** (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ) - At-Tin 95:4. "Taqwim" means not just upright posture, but the capacity to fix ourselves and reform what's around us. We can overcome our weaknesses and stand strong.

#### Category 3: How Humans Become (Our Self-Inflicted Failures)

These weaknesses were built into us, but there are qualities we *choose* to develop:

- **"The human being is extremely oppressive and ungrateful"** (إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ) - Ibrahim 14:34. Allah ﷻ gives us countless favors, yet we're unjust and deny His blessings.

"Zalum" (ظَلُوم) means someone who puts things where they don't belong. You're supposed to be patient with your children but you're impatient - you've put impatience where it doesn't belong. You should be grateful to your parents but you complain - you've put complaints where they don't belong. We oppress ourselves with wrong emotions, wrong words, wrong spending, wrong earning. We keep misplacing things.

"Kaffar" (كَفَّار) means extremely ungrateful. Allah ﷻ keeps giving; we keep being ungrateful, only focusing on what's wrong, never on what's right.

- **"The human being is extremely disloyal"** (إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ) - Al-'Adiyat 100:6. "Kanud" means someone who only sees negatives, makes lists of complaints, never reasons to be grateful.

- **"The human being calls for evil as he calls for good, and he has always been hasty"** (وَيَدْعُ الْإِنسَانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعَاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ ۖ وَكَانَ الْإِنسَانُ عَجُولًا) - Al-Isra 17:11. We want harmful things, thinking they're great, because we're in too much of a rush to think properly.

- **"The human being wants to burst right into whatever is in front of him"** (بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنسَانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُ) - Al-Qiyamah 75:5. We jump into temptation without restraint.

- **"If We gave the human being all the treasures of My mercy, he would still hold back from spending, because the human being is unnecessarily stingy"** (لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنفَاقِ ۚ وَكَانَ الْإِنسَانُ قَتُورًا) - Al-Isra 17:100. Even when we have enough, we're stingy.

- **"The human being is, in most things, extremely argumentative"** (وَكَانَ الْإِنسَانُ أَكْثَرَ شَيْءٍ جَدَلًا) - Al-Kahf 18:54. We love to argue for argument's sake. We're defensive, can't accept when we're wrong.

- **"The human being is rebellious when he sees himself as self-sufficient"** (كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَيَطْغَىٰ * أَن رَّآهُ اسْتَغْنَىٰ) - Al-'Alaq 96:6-7. We cross lines and rebel because we think we're independent, self-sustained, with no consequences.

- **"Does the human being think he'll be left aimlessly wandering?"** (أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنسَانُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى) - Al-Qiyamah 75:36. Like a camel in the desert leaping in random directions with no destination, no guidance, rushing toward nothing - until it realizes too late that it's lost in the middle of nowhere and will die. Do you think you can live with no consequences, running wherever your whims take you?

- **"We offered the trust to the heavens, earth, and mountains, and they refused to bear it - but the human being bore it. Indeed, he has been extremely wrongdoing and ignorant"** (إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا وَحَمَلَهَا الْإِنسَانُ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا) - Al-Ahzab 33:72. We accepted the burden of choice - to obey or disobey Allah ﷻ by our own will. So far, we've been extremely wrongdoing and out of control with our emotions.

- **"Even though the human being has full view of himself, even though he comes up with excuses..."** (بَلِ الْإِنسَانُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ * وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُ) - Al-Qiyamah 75:14-15. You know exactly what you're doing when you make excuses. You lie to yourself saying "I just can't" when the truth is "I won't." There's a difference. Allah ﷻ knows, and deep down, so do you.

#### Summary of the Three Categories

**What we were made from**: Dirt and fluid - humble, even humiliating origins. We forget and become arrogant.

**How we were made**: With weaknesses - impatience, anxiety, tendency to rush, constant toil. But Allah ﷻ gave remedies for each weakness. By the end, we were made in the best form (أحسن تقويم) - capable of overcoming everything and standing upright.

**How we become**: We *choose* to be oppressive, ungrateful, disloyal, negative, hasty, argumentative, rebellious, self-deluded. We know better, but we do it anyway.

These ayat make us realize: **We are in loss.** These failures - oppression, ingratitude, rebellion, self-deception - are manifestations of our loss. We weren't programmed this way; we *chose* to become this way. Which means we can choose not to be this way.

May Allah ﷻ give us the strength to make the right choices in life.

#### Category 4: Human Reactions to Good and Bad

A recurring theme: When good happens, how do we react? When bad happens, how do we react?

- **"When harm touches the human being, he calls on Me - lying down, sitting, or standing. But as soon as We remove his harm, he goes on as if he never called on Us for relief"** (وَإِذَا مَسَّ الْإِنسَانَ الضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنبِهِ أَوْ قَاعِدًا أَوْ قَائِمًا فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُ مَرَّ كَأَن لَّمْ يَدْعُنَا إِلَىٰ ضُرٍّ مَّسَّهُ) - Yunus 10:12. In crisis: "Ya Allah, Ya Allah!" In comfort: "I'm good, I'm fine." The relationship and connection disappear.

- **"When We let the human being taste mercy from Us, then We take it away - he becomes completely hopeless and extremely ungrateful"** (وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَاهَا مِنْهُ إِنَّهُ لَيَئُوسٌ كَفُورٌ) - Hud 11:9. Good times come - you enjoy them. They're taken away - you fall into despair: "Nothing is good in my life, why should I be happy?" You focus only on the negative, unable to be grateful.

- **"When We shower the human being with favor, he turns away and distances himself. When harm comes to him, he's full of extensive prayer"** (وَإِذَا أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى الْإِنسَانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَأَىٰ بِجَانِبِهِ ۖ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَاءٍ عَرِيضٍ) - Fussilat 41:51. When life is good: "Who needs God? I have everything." When difficulty hits: now the long, elaborate prayers begin.

- **"When harm comes to him, he makes dua to Us. Then when We wrap him completely in favor, he says, 'All this came to me because of knowledge I have'"** (وَإِذَا مَسَّ الْإِنسَانَ ضُرٌّ دَعَانَا ثُمَّ إِذَا خَوَّلْنَاهُ نِعْمَةً مِّنَّا قَالَ إِنَّمَا أُوتِيتُهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ) - Az-Zumar 39:49. Success? "I earned this because I'm smart." No acknowledgment of Allah ﷻ. "It's nothing but a trial, but most of them don't know" (بَلْ هِيَ فِتْنَةٌ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ).

- **"The human being never tires of asking for good things"** - but only worldly things. And when he doesn't get them? **"He becomes hopeless"** (لَا يَسْأَمُ الْإِنسَانُ مِن دُعَاءِ الْخَيْرِ وَإِن مَّسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَيَئُوسٌ قَنُوطٌ) - Fussilat 41:49. Your only relationship with Allah ﷻ is "give me this, give me that" - material requests. When you don't get what you want: "What's the point of dua? I tried it, it doesn't work."

- **"When We give the human being favor, he ignores. And when harm hits him, he makes long, elaborate prayers"** (وَإِذَا أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى الْإِنسَانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَأَىٰ بِجَانِبِهِ ۖ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَاءٍ عَرِيضٍ) - Fussilat 41:51. The hypocrisy of it: good times mean distance from Allah ﷻ, bad times mean you come running back.

- **"When We give him good, he says, 'My Master has honored me.' But when We test him by restricting his provision, he says, 'My Master has humiliated me'"** (فَأَمَّا الْإِنسَانُ إِذَا مَا ابْتَلَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ * وَأَمَّا إِذَا مَا ابْتَلَاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَهَانَنِ) - Al-Fajr 89:15-16. Material success = "Allah ﷻ loves me." Material difficulty = "Allah ﷻ hates me." We've become materialistic even in our relationship with Allah ﷻ.

If this were the attitude of the prophets, what would Nuh (AS) say after 950 years of rejection? What would Ibrahim (AS) say while homeless and expelled? What would our Messenger ﷺ say while persecuted, insulted, spit upon every day?

No. They had Iman - true Iman, both intellectual and spiritual. They never said "Allah ﷻ has humiliated me."

This category of ayat about our reactions is also our loss. We have a flimsy, shallow relationship with Allah ﷻ. The weak nature of our connection is exposed when good versus bad times hit us.

#### Category 5: Human Beings and Their Parents

Three times, the word "insan" appears specifically regarding our relationship with parents:

- **"We enjoined the human being regarding his parents"** (وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنسَانَ بِوَالِدَيْهِ) - three occurrences with variations: "His mother carried him with difficulty upon difficulty" (حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ كُرْهًا وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًا), etc.

Why does Allah ﷻ connect "insan" with parents specifically?

Because everywhere else, "insan" is used in relation to Allah ﷻ. But here, it's about parents. And I started to appreciate: **if human beings cannot be grateful to parents they can see, how will they ever be grateful to Allah ﷻ they cannot see?**

As a child, your parents are your immediate authority. They provide, protect, guide, care, remove pain, bring joy. Everything Allah ﷻ does, at a limited human level, your parents do for you first. Allah ﷻ made them a means for you to experience what He ultimately does.

If you can't be grateful to them, gratitude to Allah ﷻ becomes impossible.

This is why Allah ﷻ pairs gratitude to Him with gratitude to parents:
- **"Be grateful to Me and to your parents"** (أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيْكَ) - Luqman 31:14
- **"Worship Allah and be excellent to parents"** (وَاعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئًا ۖ وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا) - An-Nisa 4:36

The only entity Allah ﷻ places next to gratitude to Himself is parents. Your relationship with them has direct proportional impact on your relationship with Allah ﷻ.

*(Note: This requires deeper, balanced discussion. There are complex situations - abuse, trauma, neglect - that need dedicated, compassionate treatment. The fundamental teaching remains, but exceptions and nuances exist. This deserves its own comprehensive study.)*

#### Category 6: The Reality Check

The final category consists of only two ayat - the ultimate reality:

1. **"O human being! You are laboring and toiling toward your Master, and you will meet Him"** (يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ) - Al-Inshiqaq 84:6

Whether you believe or not, whether you like it or not - two things are unchangeable:
- Your life is full of difficulty and toil
- You are heading toward Allah ﷻ and will meet Him

Some people walk away from faith saying "My life is too hard, I don't have time for religion." Allah ﷻ is saying: "Your life *is* hard, and you're *still* coming to Me. That's not an excuse. It's about to get tougher because you'll meet Me."

2. **"Truly, the human being is in loss"** (إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ) - Al-'Asr 103:2

This is the ultimate reality check. These two ayat together say: Life is tough, time is running out, and you're headed to Allah ﷻ. You're in loss. That's the default state.

But there is a way out.

### The Meaning of "Khusr" (Loss)

The word "khusr" (خُسْر) appears in the Quran in three forms:

- **Khusran** (خُسْرَان): the worst possible loss
- **Khasara** (خَسَارَة): already in loss that keeps getting worse (often with "athar" - even more loss)
- **Khusr** (خُسْر): the base notion of loss

Allah ﷻ uses "khusr" here - the simplest form. This indicates there are **degrees of loss**. Some lose a lot, some lose a little. Some don't feel it, others feel it intensely. Like losing $1 - a millionaire won't notice; someone poor feels it deeply.

Whoever you are, whether you realize it or not, at some level you are experiencing loss. The ultimate loss is time itself, but it's not limited to that.

#### The Origins of "Khusr"

In Arabic, "khasir" (خاسر - loser) has profound meanings:

**First**: A cheater. Someone who tips the scales to give customers less than they paid for. This implies: **Humans are being cheated, deceived, scammed.** We're running after things thinking they'll bring success, but we're actually headed toward failure. We've been duped.

This connects to elsewhere in the Quran: **"Worldly life is nothing but means of deception"** (الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا مَتَاعُ الْغُرُورِ) - Ali 'Imran 3:185.

That's why we make this dua: **"Show us truth as truth and grant us the ability to follow it; show us falsehood as falsehood and grant us the ability to avoid it"** (اللَّهُمَّ أَرِنَا الْحَقَّ حَقًّا وَارْزُقْنَا اتِّبَاعَهُ وَأَرِنَا الْبَاطِلَ بَاطِلًا وَارْزُقْنَا اجْتِنَابَهُ). Sometimes falsehood is so attractive we can't help being pulled toward it.

**Second**: Someone who has lost their mind from losing everything - **insanity**. The image is someone running into a burning building: "What are you doing?! You're crazy!"

That's Allah's comment: humans are running toward their own destruction like madmen.

**Third**: Business bankruptcy - the collapse of an enterprise. **Humans are drowning in bankruptcy.**

**Fourth**: Losing something continuously, bit by bit by bit. Not all at once - a slow drip. Like the frog in water that's slowly heated: it doesn't jump out because death comes gradually.

This mirrors "al-'asr" - time squeezed away drop by drop.

**Fifth**: Bitterness - like sap from a tree so bitter you can barely taste it, or milk gone bad beyond tolerance.

Life becomes intensely difficult, and humans must develop toughness to survive it.

Classical scholars summarized: "They interpreted 'khusr' as destruction, retribution from Allah ﷻ, and material loss - meaning whatever humans do in this life, believer or disbeliever, they can only lose. We can only lose our youth, our health, our strength. You can have the nicest home, but by 75, you can't climb the stairs. The six bedrooms upstairs are useless. The beautiful pool becomes a danger. Young people visit and say 'I wish I lived here,' while the actual owner feels imprisoned."

**"Everyone on this earth is meant to be discontinued"** (كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ) - Ar-Rahman 55:26. **"Whoever We give long life, We reverse them in creation"** (وَمَن نُّعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِي الْخَلْقِ) - Ya-Sin 36:68. We were designed to deteriorate - that's not a flaw, it's by design. We keep getting weaker, smaller, like we were as children.

Children look up, climb up, reach up. The elderly bend down, looking toward their final home.

Children keep us up at night. The elderly can't sleep - perhaps Allah's ﷻ way of facilitating night prayer before they meet Him.

### The Quran's Commentary on Loss

**Loss is about missed opportunities.** That's how the Quran defines it. Not money, not material possessions, not even loved ones. **Loss is opportunity wasted.**

**"Had you truly believed and followed the right guidelines, you could have taken advantage of that opportunity."**

Imam Mawdudi said: "People should have been competing in things far more worthy of competition. They were competing in looks, money, showing off - what was that worth? It was incumbent on people to wake from their sleep of distraction and entertainment before all is lost, and the only thing left alive is regret."

**"When death arrives, he calls out: 'My Master, just send me back! I'll change, I promise! My time's up - just give me overtime, and I'll be different this time!'"** (رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ * لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صَالِحًا) - Al-Mu'minun 23:99-100. But that's too late. The only thing left is regret.

May Allah ﷻ not make us from those people.

### Lessons from History (الْعَصْر)

"Al-'asr" - time that passed away - reminds us of perished nations. Allah ﷻ describes them: **"Aren't you the same people who used to swear there would be no downfall for you?"** (أَلَمْ تَكُونُوا أَقْسَمْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ مَا لَكُم مِّن زَوَالٍ) - Ibrahim 14:44.

When you visit ancient ruins - Roman, Egyptian, wherever - you're not just looking at monuments. **You're standing at the graves of entire nations.** Powerful civilizations who believed "We're #1 forever, baby!"

Time ripped them to shreds. Now they're part of your selfie. That's all they are now.

**"I swear by time as it passes..."** - Allah ﷻ uses this very statement to remind us: look at that history. You're still around. Don't join them. **Learn from history and get your act together.**

### Three Roads to Reality

The Quran uses three fundamental methods to wake us up to reality:

**First: History and Time** (الْعَصْر itself). Allah ﷻ constantly references past prophets, nations, incidents. Learn from the mistakes of others; take role models from those who came before. When we become oblivious to history, one door to seeing reality closes.

The only other surah definitively called "a summary of the entire Quran" is Al-Fatihah. And even there, when we ask for guidance - **"the path of those You have favored"** (صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ) - we're looking at people across *history*. Not those who earned Your wrath, not the lost - but those You already showered with favor. We look back to find them.

**Second: Creation Around Us.** Allah ﷻ wants us to look at the sky, the earth, the bird differently. **"Have they not seen the bird above them, spreading and contracting its wings? None holds them except the Most Merciful"** (أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صَافَّاتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ۚ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمَٰنُ) - Al-Mulk 67:19. That bird reminds me of His love - He's holding it in the air out of mercy.

This isn't just intellectual "Look at creation, there must be a God." When you observe creation, you develop an **emotional bond** with Allah ﷻ. You see His mercy, His love, His planning.

Stop before you peel that orange. Where did it come from? A seed. From a tree. Picked (maybe in Brazil, California, halfway across the planet). Transported by truck, plane. Sat in a grocery store. Your mom went shopping, picked it up, brought it home. **How many journeys did Allah ﷻ employ so this fruit could reach your plate?** Even the peel - when you throw away candy wrapper, it poisons the earth. The peel of a banana or orange *feeds* the earth. It didn't have to smell good, but it does.

We could've been fed like horses munching off the ground. Instead, we're fed with delicate care. If we just contemplate the world Allah ﷻ made, we become grateful creatures. Our perspective changes.

But when we feel *entitled* - "Where's the Pepsi?! How am I supposed to eat this burger with *water*?!" - our eyes close to reality.

**Third: Allah's Words - The Quran.** The most powerful. It's only because of His word that we can fully take advantage of the first two. I wouldn't have looked at food the same way if the Quran didn't inspire me. I wouldn't think about history properly without Allah's words.

The Quran is **"a heartfelt counsel that has come to you from your Master"** (قَدْ جَاءَتْكُمْ مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ) - Yunus 10:57. Someone writes you a handwritten letter - there's sentimental value. Imagine the painstaking delivery of the Quran from the seventh heaven to you and me, from Him. That changes everything.

The more we engage with the Quran, the more our eyes open. The further we get from it, the more doors shut - even the first two.

**Important**: The first two - history and creation - we're capable of appreciating even without hearing a single word of the Quran. But after the Quran came, we have no excuse not to take advantage of all three.

When you study the Quran (especially Makkan surahs), see which avenue Allah ﷻ is using to open your eyes.

Imam Sha'rani said about this surah: **"By swearing by time, Allah ﷻ has made us look at all of history, all the strange events that have occurred, all the lessons that can possibly be drawn - and we will come to this conclusion: every human being is in loss."**

The study of human history - European, African, ancient civilizational, whatever - is inherently **Islamic**. When you study history, you're studying something that reinforces (إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ). Historians are critically important. History is critical.

The Quran calls for in-depth study of the humanities *and* the sciences. Any young student asking about Islamic studies: finish your degree first. Study psychology, sociology, anthropology, history, law - one of the humanities in depth. *Then* pursue Islamic studies. You'll have a broader perspective. That's needed.

We need a generation of students of Islam who are renaissance people - who understand human sciences deeply *and* study Islamic sciences. I have hope in this generation, insha'Allah.

## The Exception

### إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ

**"Except those who believed, did good deeds, counseled one another to truth, and counseled one another to patient perseverance."**

### The Transition to Exception

Before describing the exception, notice what the Quranic style does:

Allah ﷻ could have said positively: "Those who believe and do good deeds are not losers." That would be reassuring. But instead, He first mentions the **overwhelming loss** of humanity, *then* uses "illa" (إِلَّا - except) - making the saved seem like **exceptional people**.

Exceptions are always the minority. If they were the majority, the other part would be the exception. By using "illa," Allah ﷻ already signals: **these are very, very few people.**

It makes me wonder: Are they really that few? Can I be among them? I might have felt relief if He'd said "believers are not losers" - because I'm a believer, so I'm cool. But by making it an exception, He makes me question: Am I truly exceptional enough to be part of this?

This also shows the surah is a **minimum standard** - not for an "A grade," but for **not failing**. A teacher can say "Here's how you get an A" - but even if you don't get an A, you might get a B or C and still pass. This surah isn't interested in the A. It's saying: **"Here's the minimum requirement to not fail."**

Only those who meet these minimum requirements will pass. And these aren't casual requirements you can skip. The surah presents: **all four criteria or nothing at all.**

### Why Are Four Criteria Necessary?

In some places, the Quran mentions only: **"Those who believed"** (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا). In other places: **"Those who believed and did good deeds"** (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ). But here, it's most elaborate: **"Those who believed, did good deeds, counseled one another to truth, and counseled one another to patient perseverance."**

Think of it like a seed containing an entire tree:

- Sometimes Allah ﷻ mentions just the **seed** (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا - those who believed)
- Sometimes the **seed and what it sprouts** (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ - belief and deeds)
- Here, He mentions the seed, what it sprouts, and **its main branches** (تَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ)

What comes from good deeds necessarily includes counseling to truth and perseverance. So this surah "opens up" Iman - what are the logical consequences of true faith?

Allah ﷻ says on Judgment Day: **"No wealth or children will benefit - only someone who comes before Allah with a sound heart"** (يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ * إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ) - Ash-Shu'ara 26:88-89. What's in our heart is Iman. Iman is the only thing worth anything.

But Allah ﷻ considers these four items an **extension of Iman itself**. None of us should be confused about what true Iman looks like.

A seed could be dead or alive. If alive, it sprouts these things. If dead, it remains just a seed - actually, it's not even a seed anymore; it's dead space.

### The Critical Distinction: Our Judgment vs. Allah's Judgment

Before we go further, a fundamental distinction:

**How we judge each other**: As long as someone says "Salaam" to you, you consider them Muslim, a believer. **"Don't say to someone who throws Salaam to you that they're not a believer"** (وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰ إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلَامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا) - An-Nisa 4:94. We owe each other **benefit of the doubt**.

Imagine a Muslim man with two sons. One is very religious - prays Fajr at the masjid, prays Isha at the masjid, stays away from haram. The other son barely prays, has a drinking problem, does all kinds of wrong.

The father passes away. Time for inheritance distribution. Should the more righteous son get more because his Iman is better quality?

**No.** As far as Islamic law is concerned, both are Muslim. When their rights are weighed, you don't weigh what's in their hearts. That will be judged by Allah ﷻ. As far as we're concerned, unless someone is openly doing haram in public, we don't consider one better than the other. **The Messenger ﷺ said you cannot assume anyone's superiority over anyone else except by taqwa - and taqwa is here (pointing to the heart).** If it's in the heart, you and I can't see it. Only that person knows where they stand, and Allah ﷻ knows.

We give benefit of the doubt. We see each other as equal. We pray in the same row. Nobody's better than anyone else before Allah ﷻ. We're all the same - that's a fundamental teaching of Islam. You don't organize rows by beard size or level of knowledge or piety. If you're Muslim, you're in the row. Period.

**But**: There will be another kind of judgment - where do you *actually* stand? That cannot happen here. When will it happen? **Judgment Day.**

It may be that I called you Muslim, you called me Muslim, I called you a believer, you called me a believer - and we're shocked on Judgment Day. Allah ﷻ says: "No, you're a loser." 

"But I believed! I had Iman!"

There may be people, including ourselves, who stand before Allah ﷻ in absolute shock: *"Why am I being treated like this?"*

That young man - the "bad son" - may always be a believer as far as we're concerned. But if he goes before Allah ﷻ, Allah ﷻ may say: "You said it, but you didn't really mean it. You never truly felt it."

Some people all their lives thought they were Muslim, fine, safe. They come before Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day. A wall drops between them and the other believers. They cry out: **"Weren't we with you?"** (أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ) - Al-Hadid 57:14.

**"Yes, but you procrastinated. You didn't act when told to act. You kept doubting. You were always reluctant, deluded by false hopes. Until the decision of Allah came, and the devil got to you. So you're on the wrong side of that wall."**

This is the reality of Iman: how Allah ﷻ judges you, not how you and I judge one another.

From the Quran, when it talks about Iman and faith, it's often describing **how Allah ﷻ will judge you** - not giving you license to judge anyone else. That's critically important.

**How we judge**: Muslim? Believer? Benefit of the doubt. They have taqwa as far as we know.

**How Allah ﷻ judges**: That's between Him and them.

Allah ﷻ describes people by what they truly have in their hearts - and He addresses even *believers* saying: **"O you who believe, believe!"** (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا آمِنُوا) - An-Nisa 4:136.

Wait - "Those of you who believe, believe"? That's like saying "Those sitting, sit down." Shouldn't it be "Those who disbelieve, believe"?

No. **"O you who see each other as believers, who assumed they believe, who give each other benefit of the doubt - now it's time to actually bring that faith into your hearts in reality where nobody else will see except Allah ﷻ."**

That's the Iman, the faith, that can be very high or very low. It happens inside you and me every day. Parts of our day, Iman may be strong - our connection with Allah ﷻ, fear, love, hope may be high. Other times, low. But we're standing in the same row. Someone doesn't hover in the air because their Iman went up. They're hovering on the inside, not the outside.

**"And Allah increases them in guidance"** (وَيَزِيدُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا هُدًى) - Maryam 19:76. That increase happens inside.

This surah, when it says "except those who believed" - these are the minimum criteria of success, the minimum to avoid failure - **this is not the same as the minimum criteria by which Muslims judge each other.**

You don't get to look at someone and say, "According to Surah Al-Asr, you're a loser, because in my opinion, you don't have Iman or 'amal salih, and I've never heard you do tawasi bil-haqq or tawasi bis-sabr."

**No.** The Quran already told you: don't say about anybody who throws Salaam your way that they're anything less than a believer. Benefit of the doubt.

We don't give advice to each other? Of course we do. But we can't pass judgment on one another. **These judgments are for ourselves.**

That's a critical, essential distinction we must make.

### Part One: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (Those Who Believed)

#### Iman: Both Mind and Heart

With Allah ﷻ, our Iman is directly related to our actions - every single one. Whether you guarded your eyes or not affected your Iman. Whether you fell into temptation and earned something haram affected your Iman. Whether you lied on your resume affected your Iman. Every action affects Iman - even though to us, it didn't affect our "Islam." You still woke up Muslim.

**Those are two very distinct things.**

When Allah ﷻ says الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, don't think "those who are Muslim on the outside." This is talking about the Iman that happens **on the inside**. That's the one that even if everyone else believes you have, you still may not have. And the opposite is true: nobody else thinks you have it, but you may have it.

**Every one of these four conditions must be looked at two ways: by way of the mind ('aql) and by way of the heart (qalb).**

#### The Example of Iblis

Did Iblis believe Allah ﷻ exists? More than we do - he spoke to Him, was in His company. No doubt in his mind about Allah ﷻ, angels, Heaven, Hell. He saw that stuff. It wasn't belief in the unseen for him; it was belief in the seen.

Yet Allah ﷻ describes him: **"He was among the disbelievers"** (كَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ) - Al-Baqarah 2:34.

Was he not convinced? No - he had "tasdiq" (intellectual conviction). His problem was different: his mind accepted, but his heart refused. That's kufr by the heart, not by the mind.

Just because somebody believes intellectually doesn't mean they're a believer spiritually - that their heart is satisfied with what Allah ﷻ has to say.

When Allah ﷻ says الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, He means **both**: by way of the heart *and* by way of the mind.

#### Two Paths to Faith

Some people are emotional. When they hear good advice, their heart melts, they start crying. **"You see their eyes overflow with tears"** (تَرَىٰ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ) - Al-Ma'idah 5:83. That's the heart accepting.

Others are intellectual - they want evidence, philosophical foundations, logical proofs, rational arguments. "Why should I accept this religion? What's convincing about it?" That's fair. The Quran offers rational arguments.

**But:** Even if you came to this religion intellectually, your journey has just *started*. Now you must earn faith in your heart.

If you only have intellectual acceptance - **"The Bedouins said, 'We believe.' Say: 'You have not believed; rather say, "We have submitted," for faith has not yet entered your hearts'"** (قَالَتِ الْأَعْرَابُ آمَنَّا ۖ قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَلَٰكِن قُولُوا أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمَانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ) - Al-Hujurat 49:14.

Building Iman intellectually is one journey. Building it spiritually is *another* journey - two very different paths.

Someone becomes Muslim after an intellectual journey. They might think: "My Iman journey is done. I'm intellectually convinced Islam is truth. I'm satisfied. I never revisit that conclusion."

I've met with orientalist professors, non-Muslims, atheists - I'm never revisiting whether Islam is true. I'm not open-minded about that. I'm intellectually satisfied the Quran is the word of Allah ﷻ. That's done. I have no curiosity left about whether it is or isn't. Zero.

**But does that mean my Iman is complete?** No. That just means my intellectual Iman is satisfied. My spiritual Iman is something that must be nurtured constantly, hit hard again and again and again. That's the only way it stays alive.

Now you understand: **"O you who believe, believe!"** It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying: "Those of you who believe up here (the mind), it's time to believe in here (the heart)."

**"Is it not yet time for those who believe that their hearts should tremble at the remembrance of Allah?"** (أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ) - Al-Hadid 57:16.

This is part of the implications of الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا.

#### Another Example: Pharaoh

One of the greatest disbelievers in history, who called himself god. His ministers were aligned with him. Yet Allah ﷻ says about the undeniable miracles of Musa (AS) - the staff becoming a snake, the hand turning white, the plagues: **"They rejected them, though their inner selves were convinced, out of wrongdoing and arrogance"** (وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا) - An-Naml 27:14.

They argued against every miracle. But deep inside? Absolutely convinced.

Why didn't they accept? **"Because they wanted to continue doing wrong, and they didn't want to lose their supremacy"** (ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا).

Abu Sufyan (RA) - before he became Muslim - used to sneak to the Prophet's ﷺ house at night to listen to Quran, even though he cursed the Prophet ﷺ during the day and told young people not to listen. Three leaders did this - they'd run into each other at night: "What are you doing here?!" 

"Okay, we really want Quran, but we can't come back - the youth will find out and our campaign against Islam will die."

After he became Muslim, Abu Sufyan (RA) said someone once asked Abu Jahl: "So what's wrong with Muhammad ﷺ? Why don't you follow him?"

Abu Jahl said: "Obviously he's a messenger."

"Then what's the problem?"

"If we accept his supremacy, Banu Hashim is on top forever. We always compete with them. You want us to lose to them forever?"

Intellectually convinced? Absolutely. Problem? The heart. Even that's for disbelievers.

But even for believers, our problem with Iman often isn't intellectual - it's in the heart.

#### Modern Application

Young people today in college take philosophy courses, become impressed with scientific atheism, "new atheism." They may need rational or scientific arguments - fine.

But often, through conversation, you realize: **This isn't really about rational arguments or scientific evidence.** This is about: "Why can't I party like everyone else?"

It oozes out. Once you break apart the intellectual disagreements, the real argument emerges: **"But I don't *feel* like this is something I want to do."**

When you say "feel" and "want," you're not talking about the mind. You're talking about the heart. And **that's** all that will matter on Judgment Day.

**"It will not benefit except the one who comes to Allah with a sound heart"** (إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ) - Ash-Shu'ara 26:89.

Hameeduddin Farahi said: **Faith, when it comes to our minds, is guidance. At the same time, it is reformation and purification for the heart.**

That's what faith is supposed to be. When Allah ﷻ in this surah says الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, it's something that if it's not heartfelt, we haven't attained it. We'll still stay in loss - intellectually Muslim, but the heart hasn't submitted to Islam.

#### Our Relationship with the Quran

The Quran addresses this twice when it talks about contemplation:

**"Do they not then contemplate the Quran?"** (أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ) appears twice. One time, He mentions: if you did contemplate it, you'd be intellectually convinced. The other time: your hearts wouldn't be locked anymore.

**Contemplation on the Quran opens the mind.** Contemplation on the Quran also **opens the heart.**

This is unique to the Quran - it doesn't exist in any other document or word in human history. Usually, a document is intellectually satisfying *or* emotionally appealing. Poetry moves you to tears, but it's not profound in wisdom. Scientific works are full of knowledge, but they don't touch your heart.

The Quran can take a scholar an entire lifetime, and they're still barely scratching the surface intellectually. That same Quran can be recited, and the taxi driver, the grocery store worker, someone who barely knows anything - they can stand there and cry.

**It reaches the heart and the mind simultaneously.**

This is our book. When Allah ﷻ says الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, He's giving us both dimensions.

By the way, we are in the 'asr of human civilization - the final stage. **The Prophet ﷺ said, "Me and the final Hour are like these two fingers" (close together).** We're literally in the late afternoon of human history. The sun has gone down, the light is orange, Maghrib is coming any second. That brief time passing quickly - if you think of all human history as one day, we're in the 'asr of that day.

And in this 'asr, Allah ﷻ gave us a means to Iman He gave nobody else: **the Quran**. This is why so many Sahaba understood تَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ as "counsel one another with the Quran itself."

The Quran is not just a religious book. It's a way by which we **see**. That's why Allah ﷻ calls revelation "light." Iman is light. The Book itself is light. And Allah ﷻ calls Himself "the Light."

**"Believe in Allah and His Messenger and the light We have sent down"** (فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنزَلْنَا) - At-Taghabun 64:8.

Light isn't something you see. Light is the way by which you see. Light isn't seen -

translation: Except for those who have believed and done righteous deeds and advised each other to truth and advised each other to patience
